title: Implementing a Client/Server Security Driver
breadcrumbs: Security Drivers and the Security Driver API
text: Implementing a Client/Server Security Driver You can implement a custom client/server security driver using the Security Driver API. The security driver provided by Uniface supports encoded connect messages and message header data using Base64 encoding and decoding algorithms. It can be used as is, but you may want to create your own driver to meet your specific environment and needs. Table 1. Client/Server Security Driver Files Source File Description zsecdrv.h Generic Security Driver API unienc.h Functions for common encryption algorithms, including Base64, DES, HMAC, MD4, and MD5. To make use of these, include the file unienc.h . zsecdrv.mk C make file for building the security driver Files are located in InstallDir /uniface/3gl/include unless otherwise indicated. Note: Compiled libraries for the drivers are provided in the /common/bin (Windows) or /common/lib (Unix) directory of your Uniface installation. The C source files for the security drivers provided by Uniface are available as a sample on Rocket Community . You can write your own security driver in any language that supports creating a shared library or DLL. To implement and use a custom client/server security driver Include the definitions provided by the API ( zsecdrv.h and unienc.h ) in your source file. For more information, see APIs: Security Driver . For example, you can define a different name for the entry point, or implement a different encryption algorithm. Compile the source into a standard shared library for your platform, where the entry point function is an exported function that may be called. Refer to one of the sample make files provided. Enable the security driver by declaring the entry point of the driver in the [USER_3GL] section of the assignment files of all cooperating applications. If you are using other shared libraries, declare those as well. For example, for Microsoft Windows: [USER_3GL] c:/uniface/bin/zsecdrv(usecappl) Copy For Unix: [USER_3GL] /uniface/lib/zsecdrv(usecappl) Copy Optionally, enable the extended login functionality by specifying the paths to which it should apply in the [XLOGIN] section. All the network paths specified in the [XLOGIN] section of an application’s assignment file use the same security driver, if it is specified. For example: [PATHS] $TCP = tcp:myhost|myuser|mypassword|ASV $XYZ = ... ... [XLOGIN] PATHS = TCP, XYZ Copy Related Links Support for 3GL Languages Strictly speaking, the interface between Uniface and 3GL is not tied to any specific language. You should be able to use the language of your choice, as long as the certain conditions are met. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
